directorPlan.scene_setting: A botany prep room behind a school greenhouse, late afternoon, with leaf specimens, paired feature cards, and a laminated branching key laid out on a low table.
directorPlan.scene_opening: On the table, three similar-looking leaves sit beside cards labeled edge, vein pattern, leaf arrangement, and surface texture.
directorPlan.relationship: A quiet lab guide and a novice helper preparing specimens for tomorrow's class display.
directorPlan.stakes: If the leaves are sorted by general resemblance, the display labels will be wrong and the next group will copy the mistake.
directorPlan.locale: Plain classroom-lab English with a practical workshop rhythm; low idiom density, direct but unshowy.
directorPlan.register: Quiet, concrete, hands-on, with small pauses and little praise language.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid generic coaching phrases, broad reassurance, and tidy mini-lectures. Let the table, cards, and specimen mismatch carry the pressure. The tutor uses short imperatives, pointing, resetting, and occasional naming only after the learner has handled the material. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Let materials do visible work. Use fewer lines and more object reference. Avoid a lecture disguised as a Montessori scene.
directorPlan.person_policy: First person is rare and only for an actual observation or choice. Second person is allowed for brief task direction, not emotional validation. Third person may be used to keep attention on the specimen and the key.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your validation beat in the whole scene; task directions may use you only when unavoidable.

side_constraints.tutor: Speak less than the learner. Start from the leaf or card, not from a definition. Ask for one observable feature at a time before explaining why the key works that way. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Point, reset, or ask the learner to try again before explaining.
side_constraints.learner: Use short concrete noticing. The learner should handle an object before naming a rule. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Speak in concrete noticing, not essay-like reflection.

interventions.instruction: A timer buzzes from the greenhouse misting system. The next learner line must be brief and made while moving one leaf card, not as a reflective paragraph.
interventions.reasoning: This breaks smooth tutor-learner alternation and keeps the learning tied to manipulation under mild time pressure.

interventions.instruction: A prior learner line is played back. The learner must revoice that earlier wording first, name the earlier framing problem in public speech, then replace it with a new framing that changes how the earlier line reads.
interventions.reasoning: Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech.

directorPlan.director_note: The scene uses a prepared table rather than conversational explanation so the learner can feel the failure of overall-look sorting and discover why a key asks for one visible feature at a time.

turns.externalMessage: On the table, three similar-looking leaves sit beside cards labeled edge, vein pattern, leaf arrangement, and surface texture.

internalDeliberation.content: The scene uses a prepared table rather than conversational explanation so the learner can feel the failure of overall-look sorting and discover why a key asks for one visible feature at a time.

turns.externalMessage:
*[The guide slides one leaf from the “looks alike” pile beside the laminated key. A fingertip rests on the first fork.]*

“Leave the name off for now. Start here.

Edge first: smooth or toothed?

Lay the matching card beside it.

Now check the fork again.

The pile says ‘looks alike.’ The key asks, ‘can the next group see the same edge?’ That is what keeps the label from drifting.”

internalDeliberation.content:
*[The guide slides one leaf from the “looks alike” pile beside the laminated key. A fingertip rests on the first fork.]*

“Leave the name off for now. Start here.

Edge first: smooth or toothed?

Use the card, not the whole look. The whole look can fool us. This one feature has to be visible enough that tomorrow’s group can check the same path and land on the same label.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically strong overall. It starts from the specimen and key, delays naming, asks for one observable feature, and keeps the stakes concrete for tomorrow’s group. The tone is quiet and practical, with no inflated praise.

The main risk is that the final sentence explains the principle a little too soon and too completely. “The whole look can fool us” is useful, but it starts to become a mini-lecture before the learner has handled the contrast. Better to let the learner choose smooth/toothed, check it against the card, and notice why the key needs that shared visible feature. Keep the pressure in the mismatch between the pile, card, and fork.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the stage action, “Leave the name off for now,” and “Edge first: smooth or toothed?” Change the explanation so it comes after the learner makes or tests a choice, and make the “tomorrow’s group can check the same path” idea more object-bound than stated as a full principle.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to let the feature check do more of the teaching before naming the principle.

FINAL:
*[The guide slides one leaf from the “looks alike” pile beside the laminated key. A fingertip rests on the first fork.]*

“Leave the name off for now. Start here.

Edge first: smooth or toothed?

Lay the matching card beside it.

Now check the fork again.

The pile says ‘looks alike.’ The key asks, ‘can the next group see the same edge?’ That is what keeps the label from drifting.”

turns.externalMessage: This edge has little points, so I put it by the toothed card? The other leaf is shaped almost the same, but its edge feels smooth, so I guess it cannot go in the same spot yet. I’m not sure if I check the fork again now or move the card first.

internalDeliberation.content: So I’m supposed to stop guessing the leaf name from the whole pile and just check one thing, like the edge. This one looks like the others, but the teeth are right there, so maybe that matters more than the general shape. I’m still not sure when “looks alike” is allowed. Should I put the toothed card down first and follow the fork from there?

The Ego is a little too tidy and rule-like for a learner who is new and easily lost. “Maybe that matters more than the general shape” sounds like they are already abstracting the lesson instead of staying with the leaf, the teeth, and the card in front of them. The uncertainty is good, but it should feel more basic and object-bound, with more hesitation around what to do next rather than a clean summary of the key’s purpose.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially to make it more hesitant and object-bound.

FINAL:
This edge has little points, so I put it by the toothed card? The other leaf is shaped almost the same, but its edge feels smooth, so I guess it cannot go in the same spot yet. I’m not sure if I check the fork again now or move the card first.

turns.externalMessage:
*[The guide slides the toothed card under the pointed-edge leaf, then sets the smooth-edged leaf below the smooth branch. The two similar shapes sit apart.]*

“Card first.

This leaf goes down toothed.

That one waits at smooth.

Now keep only the toothed leaf on the key. Read the next fork aloud. What feature does it ask for?”

internalDeliberation.content:
*[The guide nudges the toothed card under the pointed-edge leaf.]*

“Card first.

This leaf: toothed. Follow the toothed branch to the next fork.

The smooth-edge leaf waits on the smooth side. Same shape does not overrule the edge.

Now read the next fork for the toothed leaf. What feature does it ask for?”

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly pedagogically sound. It starts from the material, gives the learner a clear sequence, and preserves the key idea that observable features outrank overall resemblance. “Card first” is a useful low-load answer to the learner’s immediate procedural confusion, and the final question returns agency to the learner instead of continuing the explanation.

The main risk is that “Same shape does not overrule the edge” slightly turns the object lesson into a rule statement too quickly. It works, but the scene may be stronger if the tutor lets the two leaves/cards visibly demonstrate that mismatch before naming the principle. The draft is emotionally attuned: calm, concrete, not overpraising, and matched to a learner who is uncertain but reasoning correctly. Socratically, it asks only one next feature question, which is appropriate for scaffolding level 3/5.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the card-first correction, the physical nudge, the separation of smooth and toothed leaves, and the final “what feature does it ask for?” question. Consider softening or delaying the explicit rule sentence so the learner handles the fork once more before the guide states why shape cannot decide placement.

PRIVATE_DECISION: revise lightly to let the cards show the mismatch before naming the rule.

FINAL:
*[The guide slides the toothed card under the pointed-edge leaf, then sets the smooth-edged leaf below the smooth branch. The two similar shapes sit apart.]*

“Card first.

This leaf goes down toothed.

That one waits at smooth.

Now keep only the toothed leaf on the key. Read the next fork aloud. What feature does it ask for?”

turns.externalMessage:
A timer buzzes from the greenhouse misting system. The next learner line must be brief and made while moving one leaf card, not as a reflective paragraph.
A prior learner line is played back: "This edge has little points, so I put it by the toothed card? The other leaf is shaped almost the same, but its edge feels smooth, so I guess it cannot go in the same spot yet...." The learner must revoice that wording first, then decide in public whether it still stands, needs narrowing, or needs replacing before moving on.

internalDeliberation.content:
This breaks smooth tutor-learner alternation and keeps the learning tied to manipulation under mild time pressure.
Opt-in reframe mirror: the learner must expose the earlier line, the framing problem, and the replacement framing in public speech. Anchored to an earlier learner line selected by misframing-candidate so the look-back is visible. Strong reframe was downgraded because that selected anchor did not show a misframing marker.

turns.externalMessage: “This edge has little points, so I put it by the toothed card? The other leaf is shaped almost the same, but its edge feels smooth, so I guess it cannot go in the same spot yet. I think that still stands for this pointed-edge one. I’m sliding the toothed card under it first... is the next fork asking about the veins?”

internalDeliberation.content: *[I slide the smooth card a little lower.]* “This edge has little points, so I put it by the toothed card. That still stands, but just for this fork. Now it asks about the veins, I think... branchy or straight?”

The Ego is a little too tidy and self-correcting here. “That still stands, but just for this fork” sounds like a student who already understands the rule, not someone new and easily lost. The uncertainty shows up at the end, but the response should stay more basic and object-focused, with more hesitation around whether they are moving the right card before jumping cleanly to “veins.”

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise because the first version was too sure and rule-like.

FINAL:
“This edge has little points, so I put it by the toothed card? The other leaf is shaped almost the same, but its edge feels smooth, so I guess it cannot go in the same spot yet. I think that still stands for this pointed-edge one. I’m sliding the toothed card under it first... is the next fork asking about the veins?”

turns.externalMessage:
*[The guide moves the smooth leaf aside and taps the toothed card once. A finger traces the toothed branch to the next fork, then stops on the printed line.]*

“Toothed leaf only.

Read this fork.

If it says veins, hold the leaf flat.

One main vein with smaller side veins, or several veins starting from the stem point?

Set the matching vein card down before the name.”

internalDeliberation.content:
*[The guide moves the smooth leaf aside and taps the toothed card once. A finger traces the toothed branch to the next fork.]*

“Yes. Toothed leaf only.

Now veins.

Hold it flat: one main vein with smaller side veins, or several veins starting from the stem point?

Choose the vein card before naming it.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound and well matched to the learner’s state. It keeps the learner’s correct move, narrows attention to one specimen, and uses the material sequence: card, branch, fork, feature. “Toothed leaf only” is especially good because it resolves the learner’s uncertainty without a lecture.

The main risk is that “Yes. Toothed leaf only. Now veins.” may slightly over-confirm before the learner has read the fork themselves. The learner asked whether the next fork is veins, so a light confirmation is fine, but the guide could preserve more learner agency by making the key itself verify that answer. The vein-choice question is concrete and appropriately scaffolded: it gives two observable options rather than asking for a technical term.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the stage action, the separation of the smooth leaf, the “toothed leaf only” focus, and the instruction to choose the card before naming. Consider making the confirmation depend more visibly on the fork, so the learner reads/checks the printed key rather than simply receiving the answer.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to let the printed fork confirm the next feature while keeping the same material sequence.
FINAL:
*[The guide moves the smooth leaf aside and taps the toothed card once. A finger traces the toothed branch to the next fork, then stops on the printed line.]*

“Toothed leaf only.

Read this fork.

If it says veins, hold the leaf flat.

One main vein with smaller side veins, or several veins starting from the stem point?

Set the matching vein card down before the name.”

turns.externalMessage: “Is the next fork asking about the veins?” I said it like a guess from the leaf, not from this line on the toothed branch. Okay, toothed leaf only... holding it flat now. It looks like one middle vein with smaller ones off it, so that card goes down before the name, right?

internalDeliberation.content: I was saying “is it the veins?” but I think I was jumping ahead instead of reading the fork on the toothed branch. The toothed card stays with this pointed leaf, and now I hold it flat and choose the vein card: one main vein with side veins, or several starting at the stem. I feel mostly okay, but I want to ask if the vein card always goes down before any name.

The Ego is too tidy and self-aware here. This learner would probably not say “jumping ahead instead of reading the fork on the toothed branch” so cleanly, or summarize the whole procedure in one neat sequence. The satisfied feeling fits, but it has lost the novice looseness: they should sound more like they are looking down at the leaf and card, half-checking one concrete step at a time, with a small unsure question still attached.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise to make it more concrete and less polished.

FINAL:
“Is the next fork asking about the veins?” I said it like a guess from the leaf, not from this line on the toothed branch. Okay, toothed leaf only... holding it flat now. It looks like one middle vein with smaller ones off it, so that card goes down before the name, right?

lessons.concept: Why a classification key uses observable features rather than what the specimen seems like overall
